title: SEGMENT 2 — The Clevers and Sato breakthrough: a stem cell in Matrigel becomes a gut

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The Clevers and Sato breakthrough: a stem cell in Matrigel becomes a gut
The year is 2009.
The lab is Hans Clevers' at the Hubrecht Institute in Utrecht, Netherlands.
The postdoc is a young Japanese researcher named Toshiro Sato.
And the experiment they're about to publish in Nature is one of those quiet bombshells that, in retrospect, you can point to and say: that's where the field began.
To understand why what they did was such a leap, you have to understand what people had been trying to do with intestinal stem cells for the previous several decades.
The intestine is one of the most rapidly self-renewing tissues in the body.
The lining of your gut — the epithelial layer that absorbs nutrients and produces mucus — completely replaces itself every four or five days.
Every four to five days, a whole new lining.
That's an enormous amount of cell turnover, and it's driven by stem cells living at the base of little pit-like structures called crypts of Lieberkühn.

Each crypt has a handful of stem cells at its base, and those stem cells produce daughters that migrate up the crypt walls, differentiate as they go, and eventually populate the finger-like protrusions — the villi — that stick up into the lumen of the gut.
For decades, researchers had wanted to isolate those intestinal stem cells and grow them in a dish.
They had tried, repeatedly, and they had failed.
You could get the cells out.
You could put them on plastic.
They would die.
Or they would survive but stop being stem cells.
Or they would survive as stem cells but refuse to differentiate into anything else.
Nobody could find the right conditions to keep them happy and productive at the same time.
The first piece of the puzzle came in 2007.
Clevers' lab identified a specific marker — a gene called Lgr5 — that was expressed on the intestinal stem cells and nowhere else in the crypt.
Using mouse genetics, they showed that Lgr5-positive cells, and only Lgr5-positive cells, could generate all the other cell types in the gut lining.
They were the true stem cells.
The Lgr5 marker became a flashlight you could shine on a sample of gut tissue and see, lighting up at the bases of crypts, exactly the cells you wanted to grow.
Two years later, Sato cracked the culture problem.
The recipe that worked, after a lot of trial and error, had four critical ingredients beyond the cells themselves.
Ingredient one: Matrigel.

Matrigel is a commercially produced extract from a particular mouse tumor cell line that secretes huge amounts of basement membrane proteins — laminin, collagen IV, entactin, and a stew of growth factors.
At low temperatures it's a liquid.
At body temperature it gels into a clear, soft, three-dimensional protein scaffold.
Think of Matrigel as a synthetic version of the protein matrix that, in the body, sits just underneath every epithelium and provides the substrate that the cells crawl on and attach to.
If you put a stem cell directly on plastic, it has no idea where it is — plastic looks nothing like the inside of an animal.
If you put it in Matrigel, suddenly the cell is sitting in something that, biochemically, resembles the basement membrane of a real organ.
The cell can polarize.
It can orient itself.
It can start to know which way is up.
Ingredient two: Wnt signal.
Wnt is one of the most ancient and important developmental signaling pathways.
In the gut, Wnt is what keeps stem cells stem-like — without Wnt signaling, intestinal stem cells differentiate and disappear.
So Sato's recipe added a Wnt agonist to the medium.
Without it, no stem cell maintenance.
With it, stem cells stay stem cells.
Ingredient three: R-spondin.
R-spondin is a protein that potentiates Wnt signaling — it binds to Lgr5 (the very receptor that defines the stem cells) and amplifies the Wnt signal even further.
Without R-spondin, the Wnt signal isn't strong enough to keep stem cells robust.
With it, the stem cells thrive.
Ingredient four: Noggin.
Noggin is an inhibitor of a different pathway called BMP — bone morphogenetic protein — which would otherwise push the cells toward differentiation.
Blocking BMP with Noggin keeps the cells in a more progenitor-like state.
And one more: EGF, epidermal growth factor, which promotes proliferation.
That's the basic "ENR plus EGF" cocktail — EGF, Noggin, R-spondin, plus Wnt — that became the canonical recipe for intestinal organoids and a template for every organoid recipe to come.
So here's what Sato did.
He isolated single Lgr5-positive cells from mouse intestinal crypts.
He embedded them in a drop of Matrigel.
He bathed the gel in medium containing EGF, Noggin, R-spondin, and a Wnt source.
And he waited.
What happened, over the course of a few days, was extraordinary.
The single stem cell didn't just divide.
It organized.
It produced daughters of multiple lineages — absorptive cells, goblet cells, Paneth cells, enteroendocrine cells, and more stem cells like itself.

Those cells arranged themselves into a structure with a central hollow lumen surrounded by epithelium, and from the epithelium budded out little crypt-like extensions, each with stem cells at its base and differentiated cells migrating outward.
The whole thing was a few hundred microns across, and it looked, under a microscope, like a tiny twisted-up version of a real intestinal lining.
Crypts and villus-like domains.
Differentiated cell types in the right places.
A lumen filled with mucus and dead cells being shed exactly as they would be in a real gut.

The 2009 Nature paper that reported this — title: "Single Lgr5 stem cells build crypt-villus structures in vitro without a mesenchymal niche" — is one of those papers that, when you read it now, you can feel the moment biology changed.
They had grown a piece of organ from a single cell.
Not a stem cell sphere, not a clump of cells, but an organized, multi-cell-type, architecturally correct piece of intestinal tissue.
And it could be passaged indefinitely.
You could break up the organoid, take out a few cells, replate them in fresh Matrigel, and they would grow new organoids.
You had a system that, in principle, could give you unlimited human gut tissue from a single biopsy.
The conceptual leap embedded in that experiment, the thing that mattered beyond just the practical recipe, was this: the architectural program is in the cells, not in the surrounding tissue.
The orthodoxy before 2009 had been that you needed all the supporting tissue — the mesenchyme, the muscle layers, the immune cells, the blood vessels — to build a gut.
Sato showed that you don't.
The epithelium can build itself, in the right gel with the right signals, without any of the rest of the body present.
The Lgr5 stem cell is, on its own, sufficient to generate the entire epithelial architecture.
The instructions for the organ are in the stem cell.
That's the founding insight of the organoid field.
Self-organization is real.
Cells, given a permissive environment and the right cues, will build organ structures on their own.
We don't have to figure out how to assemble organs from parts.
We just have to figure out the right conditions, and let the cells do the assembly.
In the years following, every other organoid system has come from variations on this insight.
Different tissue source, different gel, different cocktail of growth factors — but the same core idea.
Get the conditions right, the cells will do the rest.
